title: How to Build More Resilient Local-First Applications with AT Protocol Infrastructure
gist: Developer Jake Lazaroff argues AT Protocol (atproto) can serve as generic distributed-app infrastructure beyond social networking, demonstrating via three experiments (collaborative editor, CRDT to-do list, WebRTC signaling) that apps can drop their own backend (the App View) and rely only on personal data servers and relays.

insights.claim: A travel-planning app built on YJS plus a managed sync server lost multi-device sync entirely (falling back to manual file passing) about a year later when the sync provider was acquired and shut down, even though the app kept working locally.
insights.speaker: Olimpiu Pop
insights.so_what: Illustrates concrete vendor-dependency risk in collaborative local-first apps: the same server that enables sync is often the single point of failure for the whole product.
insights.locator: Lazaroff opened with a story from a travel planning app

insights.claim: Jake Lazaroff's central claim is that the AT Protocol can reduce applications' dependence on app-specific backend services by having users store their own data in personal data servers (PDSs) while apps use shared protocol infrastructure for storage, authorization, and sync.
insights.speaker: Olimpiu Pop
insights.so_what: Positions atproto as a candidate general-purpose distributed-app substrate, not just Bluesky's social-networking plumbing -- relevant to anyone evaluating protocol-level infrastructure bets versus per-app backend build-out.

insights.claim: In the standard atproto architecture, the App View -- a bespoke per-app server -- is optional, and Lazaroff identifies it as the weakest and most failure-prone point in the stack, arguing developers should build directly on PDSs and relays instead.
insights.speaker: Olimpiu Pop
insights.so_what: A concrete architectural pattern for reducing single points of failure in atproto-based apps: minimize custom per-app servers.
insights.locator: He then described atproto as an alternative architectural model

insights.claim: Lazaroff argues developers should build on shared, simple, generic, fungible infrastructure rather than per-app servers, pointing to multiple independent PDS hosts, free public relays, and community aggregation efforts as evidence atproto already supports that model.
insights.speaker: Olimpiu Pop
insights.so_what: Signals a maturing multi-host, commoditized-infrastructure ecosystem around atproto that could lower the barrier for third-party apps to adopt it as backend infrastructure.

insights.claim: A collaborative text editor built with YJS and ProseMirror wrote each YJS update as a record to the user's own PDS, using atproto effectively as a YJS sync server, but spreading document state across many records made interoperability with other apps harder.
insights.speaker: Olimpiu Pop
insights.so_what: Shows a working pattern for real-time collaborative editing on atproto, but flags a concrete interoperability cost engineers should weigh before adopting it.
insights.locator: In the first, Lazaroff built a collaborative text editor

insights.claim: A collaborative to-do list used native CRDT records (plain JSON with $type, listId, text, done, createdAt fields) with last-write-wins registers per field, and resolved same-record concurrent writes via compare-and-swap plus client-side merge-and-retry.
insights.speaker: Olimpiu Pop
insights.so_what: Provides a reusable conflict-resolution pattern for building multi-writer apps directly on PDS records without a central coordinating server.
insights.locator: The second experiment implemented a collaborative to-do list

insights.claim: In a WebRTC video-chat experiment using atproto purely for ICE signaling, conference Wi-Fi conditions forced a fallback to a TURN relay (which Lazaroff called the "TURN server of shame"), showing that even a fully decentralized signaling layer still needs centralized network-traversal infrastructure in practice.
insights.speaker: Olimpiu Pop
insights.so_what: A concrete limit on how far peer-to-peer/local-first architectures can go without centralized fallback infrastructure, relevant to anyone estimating true infra-cost savings from decentralization.
insights.locator: During the live demonstration, conference Wi-Fi conditions
